title: Cash, Cards & Digital Money
summary: What actually happens when you tap, Venmo, or swipe.
doThisToday: Next time you pay for something, notice whether physical cash actually moved, or if it was just numbers updating on a screen.

cards.prompt: Most money today isn't physical at all — it's just numbers in a database.
cards.explanation: When you check your bank balance, you're not looking at a pile of cash somewhere with your name on it. You're looking at a number your bank keeps track of on your behalf.

cards.prompt: You Venmo your friend $15 for pizza. What actually happens?
options: Physical cash moves from your bank to theirs
A number in your account goes down by 15 and a number in their account goes up by 15 — no cash moves
Venmo prints new money for the transfer
cards.explanation: It's all just numbers updating on servers. This is exactly how debit cards, direct deposit, and most banking work too — cash is only one small slice of how money actually moves.

cards.prompt: True or false: money sitting in your bank account as a digital number is just as real and spendable as cash in your wallet.
cards.explanation: Completely real. Banks are required to let you withdraw it as cash whenever you want — the digital number is backed by an actual claim on real money.

cards.prompt: Sort each payment method into Physical or Digital.
cards.explanation: Physical money is something you can literally hold. Digital money is a number your bank or an app keeps track of — both are equally real and spendable, just moved differently.

cards.prompt: The dollar wasn't always backed by trust alone.
cards.frontText: What year did the US officially stop backing the dollar with gold?
cards.backText: 1971. Before that, in theory, you could exchange dollars for a fixed amount of gold. Since 1971, the dollar's value comes entirely from trust in the US government and economy — no physical gold backing it.
cards.explanation: That single decision is why economists talk about "fiat currency" — money that has value because people agree it does, not because it's backed by a physical commodity.

cards.prompt: So what makes a dollar actually worth something?
cards.explanation: Today, the US dollar has value because people trust the US government and economy, and because everyone agrees to accept it for trade. That trust is the whole foundation.

cards.prompt: You've probably heard of Bitcoin or other cryptocurrencies. What's the basic difference from regular digital dollars?
options: There's no real difference at all
Crypto isn't issued or backed by any government — its value comes purely from what people are willing to pay for it, which makes it swing wildly
cards.explanation: No central bank or government stands behind crypto's value, which is a big reason its price can swing so much more than a bank balance. You'll dig into this a lot more in a later unit — for now, just know it's a very different animal from your checking account.

cards.prompt: Whether it's cash, a card, or an app — it's all just different ways of moving the same thing: value people agree to trust.
cards.explanation: Understanding that helps everything else click — your bank account, your first debit card, even prices themselves are all part of the same trust system.
